sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A community clinic tested messages intended to increase blood-pressure screening among adults who had not had a checkup in the past year. One message emphasized susceptibility by noting that hypertension can occur without symptoms. A second emphasized benefits by describing how early detection allows treatment before organ damage. A third combined susceptibility, benefits, and a free walk-in screening option meant to reduce perceived barriers. Two weeks later, screening rates were 18%, 23%, and 39%, respectively. Participants who received the combined message also reported the highest perceived self-efficacy for getting screened.
questions.stem: Which message condition produced the highest screening rate?

choices.D: All messages produced the same screening rate

explanations.A: Correct. The combined condition led to 39% screening, higher than susceptibility alone at 18% and benefits alone at 23%. Because it joined risk, benefit, and access information, it produced the strongest behavior change.
explanations.B: Susceptibility alone raised awareness of risk, but it produced the lowest screening rate.
explanations.C: The benefits message improved screening somewhat, but it did not match the combined condition.
explanations.D: The rates differ substantially, so equal effectiveness is inconsistent with the data.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Two-week blood-pressure screening rates after three health-message conditions.

questions.stem: The free walk-in option was designed to change which Health Belief Model construct?

explanations.B: Correct. Making screening free and walk-in reduces practical obstacles such as cost and scheduling. In the Health Belief Model, those obstacles are perceived barriers.
explanations.A: Severity concerns how serious the disease is, not access to screening.
explanations.C: Assimilation is a cognitive-development term and is unrelated to the clinic option.
explanations.D: Role strain concerns stress within a social role, not barriers to screening.

questions.stem: Why does the passage mention self-efficacy?

choices.C: Feeling capable of getting screened may help translate intention into action
choices.A: Self-efficacy means the disease is objectively severe
choices.B: Self-efficacy proves the study was blinded
choices.D: Self-efficacy eliminates the need for clinic access

explanations.C: Correct. Self-efficacy is confidence in performing the health behavior. The combined message made screening seem feasible, which can help people act rather than merely agree that screening is important.
explanations.A: Disease severity is separate from confidence in taking action.
explanations.B: The passage does not discuss blinding, and self-efficacy is not evidence of it.
explanations.D: Access still matters; the free walk-in option is part of why the behavior became easier.

questions.stem: A public-health campaign modeled on this study should most likely do what?

choices.D: Pair risk and benefit information with concrete ways to make screening easier
choices.A: Use fear information without offering a practical next step
choices.B: Focus only on rare complications and ignore access
choices.C: Assume education alone is sufficient for everyone

explanations.D: Correct. The strongest condition combined belief-related content with barrier reduction. A campaign should explain why screening matters while making the behavior easy to perform.
explanations.A: Fear or risk information alone was less effective than the combined approach.
explanations.B: Ignoring access would remove the barrier-reduction component that appeared important.
explanations.C: The data show that information alone was not the strongest strategy.

questions.stem: Which limitation should be considered before making a broad causal claim?

choices.A: The passage does not state whether participants were randomly assigned to message conditions
choices.B: The outcome was measured two weeks later
choices.C: The clinic focused on blood-pressure screening

explanations.A: Correct. Without clear random assignment, preexisting differences among recipients could contribute to screening differences. The results are useful, but the assignment procedure matters for causal strength.
explanations.B: A two-week follow-up defines the outcome window and is not itself a causal flaw.
explanations.C: Studying blood-pressure screening is not an internal-validity problem by itself.
explanations.D: Different content is the intended comparison, not the methodological concern.

questions.passage: A workplace wellness program surveyed employees about exercise readiness. At baseline, 26% were in precontemplation, 31% in contemplation, 24% in preparation, 14% in action, and 5% in maintenance. Employees then chose either a motivational interviewing session or a generic brochure. One month later, 42% of the motivational-interviewing group had moved at least one stage forward compared with 19% of the brochure group. The largest improvements occurred among employees who began in contemplation or preparation.
questions.stem: Which intervention was associated with more stage progression?

explanations.B: Correct. Forty-two percent of the motivational-interviewing group moved at least one stage forward, compared with 19% of the brochure group. That indicates greater stage progression after the interactive intervention.
explanations.A: The brochure group showed some progression, but much less than the motivational-interviewing group.
explanations.C: The passage compares two interventions and does not describe a no-intervention group.
explanations.D: The percentages differ by more than twenty points, so the interventions were not identical.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Percent of employees moving at least one stage forward after motivational interviewing or a brochure.

questions.stem: An employee thinking about exercising but not yet planning workouts would most likely be in which stage?

explanations.C: Correct. Contemplation involves recognizing a possible need for change without yet taking concrete action. That fits someone thinking about exercise but not planning or doing workouts.
explanations.A: Maintenance requires sustaining the behavior over time.
explanations.B: Action requires actively performing the new behavior, not just considering it.
explanations.D: Sensorimotor is a Piagetian stage and is unrelated to exercise readiness.

questions.stem: Why might contemplation and preparation employees improve the most?

choices.D: They were already considering change and may have been responsive to planning support
choices.A: They had already maintained exercise for years
choices.B: They were biologically unable to change without a brochure
choices.C: They had no ambivalence about exercise

explanations.D: Correct. People in contemplation and preparation are closer to action than people in precontemplation. Motivational interviewing can help resolve ambivalence and turn readiness into a plan.
explanations.A: Long-term maintenance is not the starting point described for these employees.
explanations.B: The data favor motivational interviewing over the brochure, not the reverse.
explanations.C: Contemplation often involves ambivalence, which motivational interviewing addresses.

questions.stem: A coach using the stages model should most likely do what?

choices.A: Match guidance to a person’s current readiness for change
choices.B: Give the same action plan to every person regardless of stage
choices.C: Assume precontemplation means the person is already exercising
choices.D: Skip preparation and require immediate maintenance

explanations.A: Correct. The stages model emphasizes that people differ in readiness. A useful intervention meets the person where they are rather than treating all employees as equally prepared for immediate change.
explanations.B: A uniform plan ignores the central idea of stage-specific readiness.
explanations.C: Precontemplation means not yet considering change, not already exercising.
explanations.D: Maintenance is a later stage and cannot simply be required at the outset.

questions.stem: Which finding would best show that the intervention changed exercise behavior, not just stated readiness?

choices.B: Activity tracker data showed increased weekly exercise minutes in the motivational-interviewing group
choices.A: Employees said the brochure was printed clearly
choices.C: The baseline survey listed five stages

explanations.B: Correct. Objective or behavioral evidence of increased exercise would show that stage movement translated into action, strengthening the claim beyond self-reported readiness alone.
explanations.A: Clear printing does not show behavior change.
explanations.C: Listing the stages describes the measure but does not validate actual exercise behavior.
explanations.D: Workout-time preference may be useful, but it does not show increased exercise.
